content.subtitle: Uses
items: Telling jokes and other informal stories

content.subtitle: Examples
items: She never **checks** her email before noon.
My uncle **runs** a small bakery in the town centre.
So, a woman **walks** into a bookshop and asks for a book on confidence ...
Torres **shoots**! And it's in! What a goal!
The documentary **explores** the hidden world of deep-sea creatures.
You **take** the second turning on the left and the station is right in front of you.
The summit **begins** on the 15th of next month.
I'll let you know as soon as I **hear** back from them.

content.subtitle: Emphatic Present Simple
content.description: To emphasise contrast or strong feeling.
examples: She may not enjoy cooking, but she **does make** an excellent pasta.
I **do wish** you'd stop interrupting me when I'm talking!

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Temporary situations and series of actions
Annoying or amusing habits (usually with always)
Background information in jokes and other informal stories

content.subtitle: Examples
items: The chef **is preparing** the sauce for tonight's special dish.
We **are studying** Victorian literature in class this term.
I **am not attending** the gym while the renovations are taking place.
Fewer and fewer people **are relying** on printed maps to navigate.
My flatmate **is always leaving** the bathroom in a mess!
A woman walks into a bookshop. She **is carrying** a long list of titles she wants ...
**Are you presenting** your research at the conference next week?
I'll probably feel more relaxed once I **am sitting** on the plane.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Situations and states that started in the past and are still true
A series of actions continuing up to now
Completed actions at a time in the past which is not important or relevant
Completed actions where the important thing is the present result

content.subtitle: Examples
items: She**'s worked** as a marine biologist for over a decade.
He**'s completed** three marathons, two triathlons and a charity swim this year.
**Have** you ever **visited** a country where you didn't speak the language?
The government**'s announced** a new policy on renewable energy.
I've just heard the most incredible piece of news.
Give me a call once you**'ve landed** at the airport.

content.subtitle: Note on American English
content.description: In American English, the past simple is often used instead of the present perfect simple.
examples: US: **Did** you **finish** the report yet? UK: **Have** you **finished** the report yet?
US: I already **sent** the email. UK: I**'ve** already **sent** the email.

examples.note: In informal American English, gotten is sometimes used as a past participle instead of got when it means 'obtain', 'become' or 'move'.

examples: US: I **haven't gotten** the package yet. UK: I **haven't got** the package yet.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Actions and situations continuing up to the present (or just before the present)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: We've **all been debating** where to hold the end-of-year party and still can't agree.
I won't apply for the promotion until I've **been working** here for at least eighteen months.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: We usually use the present perfect simple to specify a particular number of times/things. We usually use the present perfect continuous to emphasise the duration of an action/situation.
examples: ✓ I've **sent** three follow-up emails this morning.
✓ I've **worked here** for three years. (no emphasis)
✓ I've **been working** here for three years. (emphasises the duration)

sections.title: Stative and Non-Stative Uses of Verbs

content.subtitle: Stative Verbs (generally used in simple tenses)
Watch out!
content.description: Many of these verbs can be used in continuous tenses when they describe actions rather than states. Examples: appeal, be, consider, depend, feel, have, include, look, mean, mind, see, smell, taste, think, weigh.
examples: ✓ I **think** it's essential to back up your files regularly. (state: think = believe)
✓ I**'m thinking** of taking a gap year before university. (action: think = consider)

sections.title: A: Present Simple vs. Present Continuous
sections.instructions: Write the verb in brackets in the correct form, present simple or present continuous, in each gap.

items.sentence: My sister ...... (study) abroad this year so I ...... (see) her very often, unfortunately.
The train ...... usually ...... (arrive) five minutes late.
I'm sorry to hear that your company ...... (lose) money at the moment.
You ...... (take) the motorway until junction 12 and then ...... (follow) the signs for the city centre.
Why ...... oil ...... (float) on water?
It's wonderful now that the days ...... (get) warmer, isn't it?
My neighbour ...... (drink) coffee – she only has herbal tea.
Actually, we ...... (listen) to jazz at all – this is a blues band from New Orleans.
A man ...... (walk) into a dentist's. 'I'd like some teeth removed,' he says. 'Certainly, sir. Which ones?' 'All of them, please – they're driving me crazy!'
...... you ...... (call) me from work? I can hear a lot of background noise.
...... you ever ...... (shop) at that new market on Sundays?
There's a doctor, a lawyer and a pilot. They ...... (wait) at an airport when their flight ...... (get) cancelled ...
Fernández ...... (take) the corner ... it's a brilliant ball in ... and Johnson ...... (head) it home!
LOCAL COMPANY ...... (donate) THOUSANDS TO FOOD BANK
My boss ...... (always / send) emails at midnight! It's really stressful.
'Lena ...... (wear) perfume, does she?' 'Actually, she ...... (wear) the same scent every day.'

sections.title: B: Fill in the Gap (Common Phrases)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Why do you ...... forget to lock the door? One day someone is going to walk straight in.
About thirty employees are working on this project at ...... .
My brother is revising for his final exams at the ...... .
I'm perfectly fine renting for the time ...... , but I'd like to buy something eventually.
It's true that he visits us quite ...... , but he does call every week.
We go hiking ...... now and then, but only when the weather is good.
She spends ...... of her free time working on her novel.
The children ...... go to bed until they've brushed their teeth. It's a strict rule.
I bump into my old colleague from ...... to time, usually on the way to work.
I'm sitting on the beach ...... now, enjoying the sunshine.
That's the last ...... I order food online without checking the reviews first.
Do teenagers these ...... spend more time online than watching television?
She checks her messages ...... she has a spare moment, which is constantly.
I'm staying in ...... now, but I might join you later this evening.
...... is the last time I go to that hairdresser's. My fringe is completely uneven!

sections.title: C: Dialogue Completion
sections.instructions: Write a verb from the box in the correct form, present simple or present continuous, in each gap.

items.sentence: Lisa: Hey, Tom! Long time no see! What (1) (you) ...... these days?
Tom: Hi, Lisa! Great, thanks! (2) (I / currently) ...... a new mobile app for a startup in the city.
Lisa: Wow! (3) (how / it) ...... so far?
Tom: Really well, actually. (4) (it / come) ...... together nicely. The platform (5) (I) ...... at today looks really promising.

Lisa: Brilliant! Actually, the reason (6) (I) ...... you is that my (7) (colleague) ...... past your office every day on the way to lunch, and (8) (she) ...... she can drop off those documents for you.
items.answer: I'm contacting, colleague walks, she says

items.sentence: Tom: That's very kind, but actually (9) (it / not / take) long to pick them up myself, so (10) (she / not / need) to bother. Please thank her, though!
items.answer: it doesn't take, she doesn't need

items.sentence: Lisa: Of course! Tom: By the way, (11) (what) ...... with your photography project? (12) (you) ...... a venue yet?
Lisa: Not yet! (13) (I / not / even) ...... a theme yet! (14) (I / not really / focus) ...... on it right now. (15) (I) ...... to get the client presentation finished first.
items.answer: I don't even have, I'm not really focusing, I'm trying

items.sentence: Tom: Why (16) (you) ...... something on street photography? The city is beautiful at night.
Lisa: No, (17) (I) ...... urban settings! I'd rather do something (18) (that) ...... more on nature.
Tom: Like what? Lisa: Like wildlife in the local wetlands. Tom: (19) (that) ...... amazing. Lisa: (20) (you) ...... so? Great, I think I'll go with that, then.

sections.title: D: Stative Verbs
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: Could you explain what 'serendipity' **means / is meaning**, please?
The concert tickets **only cost / are only costing** twenty euros this week because it's an early-bird offer.
It **doesn't matter / isn't mattering** which of the topics you covered this semester you choose for your presentation.
It's only a short call as **I ring / I'm ringing** from the office phone.
I think **I recognise / I'm recognising** that voice. Wasn't she on that podcast last week?
Excuse me, **does the soup contain / is the soup containing** shellfish? I have an allergy.
**I really don't believe / I'm really not believing** that social media is entirely to blame for teenage anxiety.
Why **don't you play / aren't you playing** the piano tonight, Sofia? I thought you had a recital tomorrow.
Leave it, David – this **doesn't concern / isn't concerning** you in the slightest.
You really **resemble / are resembling** your mother when you smile like that.
If **you're not using / you don't use** the printer, please switch it off!
The account you have selected **does not exist / is not existing**. Please check and try again.
I need everyone to attend the briefing, and that **includes / is including** you too, Marcus!
**We're having / We have** the kitchen refitted, so it's easiest if we eat in the dining room.
At the moment, my role **involves / is involving** a lot of cross-departmental coordination.

sections.title: E: Stative vs. Active
sections.instructions: Write a verb in the correct form (present simple or present continuous) in each pair of sentences.

items.sentenceA: A I ………………… for a jacket in a medium. Have you got anything in navy?
items.sentenceB: B The new policy ………………… very complicated to me. Can someone explain it?

items.sentenceA: A How ………………… you ………………… about moving to a different city for the job?
items.sentenceB: B Carlos ………………… rather dizzy, so I've suggested he sit down for a moment.

items.sentenceA: A The committee ………………… introducing a new grading system.
items.sentenceB: B Why ………………… you ………………… him to be a better director than her?

items.sentenceA: A We ………………… a video call right now – can I phone you back this afternoon?
items.sentenceB: B ………………… the Petersons ………………… a second car?

items.sentenceA: A The idea of a long weekend in the mountains really ………………… to me.
items.sentenceB: B Authorities ………………… for anyone with information about the missing cyclist.

items.sentenceA: A This milk ………………… a bit off. I think you should check the date.
items.sentenceB: B Why ………………… you ………………… all the bottles before putting them in the bin, Mum?

items.sentenceA: A I ………………… on you to keep this confidential. Please don't let me down.
items.sentenceB: B Whether we finish on time ………………… on how quickly the team works.

items.sentenceA: A She ………………… a bit distracted lately, don't you think?
items.sentenceB: B The kids ………………… really noisy again! Could you ask them to keep it down?

items.sentenceA: A ………………… you ………………… the new intern would be a good fit for the marketing team?
items.sentenceB: B James said he ………………… about switching careers entirely.

items.sentenceA: A I hear that Emma ………………… quite a lot of her cousin these days. Is that right?
items.sentenceB: B I ………………… you've redecorated – the living room looks fantastic.

sections.title: F: Error Correction (Present Perfect Simple)
sections.instructions: Each of the words or phrases in bold is incorrect. Rewrite them correctly.

items.sentence: She **been** a member of the choir since she was twelve years old.
How many novels has Patrick **wrote** since his debut?
They still **not** managed to reach an agreement.
I'm never been to South America – what's it like?
Have you heard? Mia's **fracturing** her wrist at the gym.
Great timing – I've just **came** out of the meeting.
Why **you haven't tell** anyone about the changes to the schedule?
Our family is **running** this restaurant for three generations and we have no plans to close.

sections.title: G: Present Perfect Simple vs. Continuous
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: They've **collected / been collecting** over forty signatures for the petition already.
Have you **followed / been following** that series on streaming about the missing explorer? Apparently, they've just **released / been releasing** the final episode.
We've **visited / been visiting** the Louvre, the Eiffel Tower and Montmartre, and we've only been in Paris for one day!
It's the first time I've **tasted / been tasting** authentic Thai street food.
My colleagues and I have **talked / been talking** about organising a team trip for months but we can't settle on a destination.
You can't need a break already – we've only **worked / been working** for about twenty minutes so far!
I've **queued / been queuing** outside this shop for over an hour and my feet are aching!
Has Rachel **lived / been living** in that neighbourhood since she first moved to the city?

sections.title: H: Present Perfect Simple vs. Continuous
sections.instructions: Write the verb in brackets in the correct form, present perfect simple or present perfect continuous. Use contractions where possible.

items.sentence: ...... (I / reply to) over a dozen enquiries this morning already.
Is it the first time ...... (you / ever / try) sushi?
...... (we / wait) for the technician for almost three hours. Where ...... (he / be)?
items.answer: We've been waiting, has he been

items.sentence: ...... (you / just / get) a message from the head office?
I'm afraid ...... (we / run out of) coffee. ...... (we / wait) for a delivery since yesterday morning but ...... (they / turn up) yet.
items.answer: we've run out of, We've been waiting, they haven't turned up

items.sentence: ...... (I / put together) a shortlist of candidates but ...... (I / get) very far yet. ...... (you / have) a chance to look at the applications?
items.answer: I've been putting together, I haven't got, Have you had

items.sentence: Oh, Nina, my ...... (colleague / mean) to ask you for ages. Could you cover my shift on Friday?

sections.title: I: Common Adverbs
sections.instructions: Write a word from the box in each gap.

items.sentence: I've ………………… noticed what's been bothering me! The font on this poster is slightly off-centre.
My grandfather's been in and out of hospital …………………, so we're taking turns to visit him.
We've had solar panels on the roof ………………… last spring.
Have you seriously packed ………………… ? We don't leave until tomorrow morning!
We've won every home game we've played ………………… far this season.
I've always rented rather than owned ………………… now, so buying feels like a big step.
We can't back out now – I've ………………… confirmed the booking!
How long has Sam been living abroad ………………… ?
Don't get Jess that novel; I'm fairly sure she's read it ………………… .
Have you ………………… thought about writing your own blog?
We haven't chosen a name ………………… , so all suggestions are welcome.
We've never been to New Zealand ………………… , so we're incredibly excited about the trip.
Have they been renting in this area ………………… they left their previous flat?
I ………………… haven't found a solution to the problem. It's really frustrating.
I've been searching for a reliable plumber like this ………………… months!
What a coincidence you called – we've ………………… been talking about you.
Daniel, haven't you revised for tomorrow's test ………………… ? You really should get started.
